data.title: Microsoft, Amazon, big tech set to 'accelerate' deals with Khan departure: Wedbush
data.description:
Christmas has come early for big tech.

That's the prevailing sentiment from Wedbush Securities amid the news that President-elect Donald Trump named Andrew Ferguson to lead the U.S. Federal Trade Commission and replace Lina Khan as its chair. And while Ferguson is likely to have a "keen eye" on the tech world, he is expected to roll back much of Khan's "head scratching anti-tech agenda," Wedbush said.

"In essence, Christmas came early for the tech world as the Khan overhang is removed for the tech world at a key time in the AI arms race in which we expect the strong to get stronger as Mag 7 gets the engines started up again on M&A with Microsoft (NASDAQ:MSFT), Oracle (NYSE:ORCL), Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL), Amazon (NASDAQ:AMZN), and Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA) set to accelerate deals to expand their technology moat," Wedbush analysts led by Dan Ives wrote in a note to clients.

With Ferguson seen as "pro-innovation" (according to Trump's announcement), this is a "huge positive for the tech world," Wedbush analysts said. Additionally, the chance of breaking up tech companies goes down "significantly" with Khan's departure, the analysts added.

"The DOJ investigations remain and will be a battle against tech in the courts but let's be clear, today is a great day for the tech world and Silicon Valley with Khan finally gone from the FTC," the analysts explained.

Wedbush previously said that the recent antitrust investigation by the FTC against Microsoft is "not a worry" with Khan leaving the government agency.

data.title: CPI inflation picks up in November, in line with consensus
data.description:
• November Consumer Price Index: +0.3% M/M vs. +0.3% consensus and +0.2% in October.
  • +2.7% Y/Y vs. +2.7% consensus and +2.6% prior.
  • Core CPI (excludes food and energy): +0.3% M/M vs. +0.3% consensus and +0.3% prior.
  • +3.3% Y/Y vs. +3.3% consensus and +3.3% prior.

data.title: Hershey tumbles after Mondelez announces $9B share repurchase authorization
data.description:
Hershey (NYSE:HSY) sunk 5% in premarket trading after Mondelez (MDLZ) announced it approved a new share repurchase of as much as $9 billion of its stock. Mondelez rose 3.3%.

Shares of the iconic American chocolate maker fell as a Mondelez (MDLZ) share repurchase plan appears to lower the likelihood that Mondelez (MDLZ) would acquire Hershey. Mondelez also said that it's focused on bolt-on acquisitions, while Hershey has a makrt cap of $38 billion.

"The company remains committed to an acquisition strategy that is focused on bolt-on assets similar to recent acquisitions of Chipita, Clif and Ricolino," Mondelez said in its statement on Wednesday.

Hershey (HSY) shares jumped 11% on Monday after Bloomberg reported that Mondelez (MDLZ) was evaluating a potential takeover of the chocolate maker. Mondelez made a preliminary approach to Hershey about a possible deal.

data.title: OPEC cuts forecast of oil-demand growth for 2024, 2025
data.description:
OPEC on Wednesday lowered its forecast of oil demand growth for this year and next for a fifth straight month. The cut was the cartel's biggest reduction to the 2024 outlook yet after its members agreed to prolong curbs on production.

The Organization of Petroleum Exporting Countries lowered projections for consumption growth in 2024 by 210,000 barrels a day to 1.6 million barrels a day. It has cut estimates by 27% since July.

The OPEC+ alliance led by Saudi Arabia and Russia last week agreed for a third time to delay plans to restart halted crude production. They also slowed the pace of increases for next year. The first in a series of hikes was delayed to April from January in its schedule.

Crude prices have slumped 17% since early July amid weak demand from China and growing supplies in the Americas. Brent futures are at $73 a barrel, which is considered too low for the Saudis and many others in the organization to cover government spending, Bloomberg News reported.

data.title: Monolithic gets initiated with bullish views at Citi
data.description:
Citi began coverage of Monolithic Power Systems (NASDAQ:MPWR) with a Buy rating and a $700 price target.

The analysts said that following the recent sell-off due to potential share loss at Nvidia, Monolithic's premium to the Philadelphia Semiconductor Index (SOX) index has fallen to a 10-year low of about 20% to 30%. The analysts noted that they see this as an attractive entry point for investors looking for a high-quality analog semiconductor company that has consistently achieved over 20% sales and over 30% EPS compound annual growth rate, or CAGR, over the past decade.

The analysts believe Monolithic can sustain more than 20% sales growth, given the upcoming analog inventory replenishment, and also think that the fast-growing Al and Auto end markets can compensate for the company's potential share loss.

Citi noted that the price target of $700 is in line with the multiple range it was trading before the Al upturn.

Last month, Monolithic said that it has no performance issues with its products and that it was still in Nvidia's (NVDA) next-generation systems.

In November, Loop upgraded the shares of the company — which sells semiconductor-based power electronics solutions — to Buy from Hold with a $660 price target.

data.title: GFG Resources raises C$3.71M from 28.56M warrants to accelerate exploration programs in Timmins
data.description:
• GFG Resources (OTCQB:GFGSF) has raised C$3.71M from exercising 28.56M warrants expiring December 9, 2024.
  • In connection with the exercise of warrants, Alamos Gold (NYSE:AGI) has exercised 5.34M warrants for a total consideration of C$0.69M.
  • Currently, the company states that no warrants remain outstanding.
  • "This funding enables GFG to accelerate the exploration programs at our projects in the highly prospective Timmins Gold Camp. We are currently focused on drilling at our Aljo Gold Project and look forward to initiating our first-pass drill program at the Muskego Gold Target in early 2025," said Brian Skanderbeg, President and CEO of GFG.

data.title: HSBC revamp may slash $3B in costs - report
data.description:
HSBC (NYSE:HSBC), Europe's largest bank, is considering restructuring plans that could cut costs by at least $3B under its new CEO Georges Elhedery, according to a media report.

The bank's managers were told that the cuts, amounting to ~10% of the company's expenses, would take until June 2025 to complete, Bloomberg reported, citing people familiar with the matter.

This year, HSBC's (NYSE:HSBC) expense bill is expected to be ~$32.6B, according to figures compiled by Bloomberg. The restructuring plans' details, including a one-time charge, are expected to be unveiled when the company releases its full-year results in February.

The plans entail reducing the ranks of the top managers — now at 175 — by more than 40%, one of the people told Bloomberg.

Elhedery has already reduced HSBC's (HSBC) group executive committee by about a third, the report said.

HSBC (HSBC) stock rose 0.9% in U.S. premarket trading on Wednesday.

data.title: Cousins Properties announces public offering to raise ~$284M
data.description:
• Office REIT Cousins Properties (NYSE:CUZ) has priced an underwritten public offering of 9.5M shares of common stock to raise gross proceeds of ~$284M.
  • The offering is expected to close on December 12.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Chart Industries authorizes $250M share buyback program
data.description:
• Chart Industries (NYSE:GTLS) said its board has authorized a share repurchase program of up to $250M of its common stock.
  • "We anticipate that we will be at $3 billion of debt by the end of 2025, and look ahead at various opportunities to return value to shareholders, continue to pay down debt, and deploy capital for productivity and growth opportunities," CEO, Jill Evanko said.

data.title: Macy's in charts: Bloomingdale comp sales growth of 3.2%, Bluemercury comp sales up 3.3%
